tokens: We can definitely see the light mantle as it comes out over the valley here, and we're looking at Hole-in-the-Wall, although it's still too subtle.
We're looking right at Lara, as a matter of fact.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: There's Lara, very clear; and Hole-in-the-Wall, you can see it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: There's Horatio way over there where those blocks are.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: There's a little depression we didn't talk about, though, that's between Horatio and Camelot.
But it's a depression and not a blocky crater at all.
As a matter of fact, the total block population has changed.
Once we get away from the rim of Camelot, the block frequency is quite a bit smaller.
It's down, maybe to less than 1 percent of the surface.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Much easier driving with the Rover.
(Pause) Boy, am I glad we got that fender on.
Very obvious that the Rover navigation (is easier here) because of the (scarcity of) blocks and because of the smaller (static) craters, and very subtle type craters in this area.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: There are up to 2-meter, bright-halo, blocky craters - and that's blocky-walled craters...That may be instant rock rather than - I think it is (instant rock) rather than bedrock - (surrounding the 2-meter craters) in the rim area of Camelot.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Horatio has got to be...There's Horatio, right there.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let me give another mark on the southern rim of Horatio.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The Scarp looks very smooth from here.
(There are) no obvious outcrops at this time.
(Pause) (The craters) don't seem to be penetrating to any bedrock in the area we're traversing now, just to the southeast of Horatio.
Horatio has a blocky wall; however, the upper several tens of meters, probably, of rim look as if it's either mantled - or composed of - the light-gray regolith material we've been driving on.
The blocks do not come to the rim of Horatio.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I don't know if I want to take you down there or not.
Yeah, Jack, hold on; I'll take you down there.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Horatio has quite a different appearance than Camelot.
And that's the main one (that is, the main difference between Horatio and Camelot is that, at Horatio), the blocks do not get to the rim.
(Laughs) I know it's not much, but it seems like a lot.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I've just got to go around that crater.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Pause; the following has been lightly edited for clarity) It looks like - if Horatio is any gauge, and this is a wild guess, Bob - but maybe an average of 20 or 30 meters (of) stratigraphic thickness (of regolith) lies above the exposures of the subfloor - (the) exposures being blocks in the wall.
And some of those blocks, again, are several meters, if not 5 to 10 meters in diameter.
And they're concentrated on the west wall...There are very few blocks on the east, north, and south walls of Horatio.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're on the southern rim; 078, 2.3, and 2.0.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're maybe 100 meters south of the rim.
Actually, we're on the rim crest.
We're 100 meters south of the break in slope into the crater.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: It's an undulating, hummocky traverse terrain in there, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: These little craters make it bumpy; but, other than that, it's really smooth sailing!
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: This is what I sort of expected the dark mantle to look like, rather than what we landed on.
Not more than one percent of the surface (is covered with blocks), and that percentage continues right over the rim crest of Horatio down onto the wall until you hit the big blocks.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That's not...No, we're not to Bronte yet.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: No, I don't have any...No, we're not at Bronte...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And how about an amps and a mobility...(correcting himself) a speed reading.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I've been pushing anywhere from 9 to 11 clicks, and most of the time that's full out, and amperes are bouncing around 100 apiece.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Because I've been full bore most of the time, and all I can get out of it is 10 clicks; and when I decelerate, she decelerates in a hurry.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, I'm sitting on 080 right now and 2.6.
I think we've got to add a little bit to that if they're right.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We'll get a new correction for you guys on that shortly.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We see no craters that seem to penetrate into bedrock out in here - that is, with blocky rims - and that's quite a contrast to the area we sampled at Station 1A yesterday.
I cannot see in my field-of-view any blocky-rim craters.
There are slight craters with fragmental walls and rims, but it looks like instant rock rather than the subfloor material.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack, can you see over there to the left - I'll turn a little bit (for a better view) - on the dark area of the South Massif where you get those impressed lineations?
See them going from left upward to the right?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I see what you mean; right.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That's what I saw out my window.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, they go obliquely up the slope.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: They're more like wrinkles, they're linear wrinkles.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Crenulations, you might say, in the slope that look something like those I saw from orbit, looking in the shadowed area...or, at the edge of the shadows.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Bob, we've seen craters as much as 20 meters - maybe 30 meters - in diameter without blocky rims.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: The rim block population is not much different than the average for the terrain in here.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: If we can't recognize a change in that albedo when we get onto that white mantle, I'm going to be surprised.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The light mantle is just what Gene has said, it's...That's it, right now.
There are some very bright craters in it.
They stand out...Bright-haloed craters scattered over it that seem to be quite a bit brighter than anything we have out here on the dark mantle.
That's the first different-colored blocks I've seen; they're sort of...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Over to the right a little bit.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: There's a crater with a big mass of block in the bottom.
It looks like it might be a secondary fragment from somewhere.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Do you want to get a photo as we go by?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Can you swing a little bit to the right?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That might be worth a...How's our time for traverse, Bob?
Do we have time for an (extra) LRV sample?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're looking for that first LRV sample at about 4.2; that's in the light mantle.
(Get the sample here) if you can do it quickly; but we weren't planning on it.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Swing a little bit to the right now.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Right up across that little ray.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And I'll try to get a chunk of whatever (formed the crater)...Okay, I want...Keep going; keep going...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Let me get the (Rover power) switch off.
(Pause) And, Bob, I've been making 10 to 12 clicks coming across the surface; and, as I say, for the most part, that's full bore except where I have to do some rapid changes.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And, by and large, the back room is interested in you guys pressing on to Station 2.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: That's a pretty big rock in there.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: It's got quite a bit of dirt in it.
(To Bob) I think this is a block from a linear-strewn field of very irregular and jagged rocks that are southwest of a crater that's 10 to 15 meters in diameter.
It looks like the material that may have formed the crater, and you can look at some of the pictures and make up your own decision.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (To Gene) Can you get it in there?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: The bag (that is, the SCB)'s not open.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: ...And, you got a frame count, Jack?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: And I did get my "locator" here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'd better slow down my picture taking.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're in a little area where the fragment population may be up to 3 percent.
It's getting a little more like what we saw around the LM.
In fact, I would say it was comparable now.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'm going down this slope and up the other side, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: But nothing like (the block coverage at) Station 1.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And the next planned Rover sample will be at a distance of 4.2; so, 080 and 4.2.
And it will be in the light mantle if...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: It's in the first prong of light mantle, as I recall.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Your heading at 260 looks like it's right on, by the way, from what I see on the skyline.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And how's the low-gain antenna holding up?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Responding to Bob) Well, I'm moving it, so I guess you're getting it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Bob, the blocks I see still seem to be the gabbro, except for that one sample we took, which I hope was what I thought it was...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We got to get around here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I got to go around this thing.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I got to go back here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Static clears)...blocks, grayer than the normal gabbro we've seen, that have very large, egg-sized vesicles in them.
(Pause) Watch it, you got one on your right there...Here you go.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That's all right, because some of those down-Suns are hard to see.
I want to get off this slope.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I wonder if I took a picture of that block field?
Getting to be so automatic that I'm not sure what I'm taking any more.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'm going to go through this niche on a high point in the saddle here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I think we're just north of Bronte.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That seems to be about where you should be on the map here.
We gather you're circumnavigating a little bit.
Comm's dropping out from time to time.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: 080, 3.5, and 2.9; and we're on the north side of Bronte.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And it looks like Bronte has penetrated the dark mantle in here.
It got the subfloor, but there's not an awful lot of blocks around the rim.
There are just some small ones...(small) compared to what we saw around (Camelot)...Watch it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (It's nothing compared with) what we saw around Horatio or in the walls of Horatio and around Camelot.
Nothing, also, like we saw yesterday at Station 1.
(Pause) Bob, that characteristic little dimple in the bottom of the craters is still with us, and it's invariably glass-lined in the fresh ones.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Now, that's not a complete lining.
It just seems to be glass agglutinates, if you will, that's holding the fragments in the bottom of the crater together.
There's one on the side of an older crater.
We're back into about a one percent (block) coverage.
I suspect that the reason our block population went up there was because of Bronte.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: An awful lot of these smaller, glass-lined little craters around.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, and you notice, Gene, what I was saying about the little dimple in the bottom?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Watch the fresh ones, and (you'll see that) they all have that little dimple as if that...You see, there's one right there.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack, you know, I think the white mantle is starting right over there.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The place you can really see it is where it's reflected off the slopes of the...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: ...of the (Scarp) cliffs out there, but I think...I hate to say it, but Charlie may be right.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, Geno, one thing that may distinguish it is (that) the bright-halo craters are brighter.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: But I can see it from here...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: ...on the floor of the valley here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: On the scarp it really shows up.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Block population is unchanged; still appears to be - where I can see large enough blocks - the gabbro, although there's not as much to look at now in terms of blocks.
The surface characteristics have not changed.
There are no craters that we see that are bringing up clear (that is, unmistakably) blocky rims.
Most of the fresh craters have instant rock around them.
(At) craters of the same size that are older and more subdued, that instant rock is apparently broken down.
I suspect the small zapping breaks that down fairly quickly.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You still making about 9 to 11 kilometers?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I've been making from 10 to 12, Bob - mostly 12.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Can you give me a reading on the amps this time, Geno.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I've got a little navigating to do.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'm read...I'm reading...I'm reading 100 - bouncing - around 100 on both of them.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: How about amps and not amp-hours?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: And I'm going up-and-down, hummocky terrain, I think with...(Responding to Bob) Oh, I'm sorry.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Watch your...You got a hole in front of you.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, let me get up here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It takes a little getting used to, though.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'm not sure I want to go through many of those.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'll give you an amp reading as soon as I can.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Would you believe my camera handle's come off?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: The terrain gets a lot more locally hummocky with some well-rounded rims but very large-aspect-ratio craters - which you got to get around in here - in the 4- or 5-meter size.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Charlie, that...(Amazed at his slip of the tongue) "Charlie!" I was thinking of white mantle.
That's the white mantle we're coming up on right up here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That's it, there's not going to be that much difference.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Not going to be that much difference, but...(Warning himself) Look where you're going.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I got to watch I don't lose my camera.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: See, now you can look where we're going to come up on the white mantle.
It's dusted with that light...Look at it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're only 100 meters from the light mantle.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: How about giving us the range and bearing when you get to it.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Look at this crater in here.
(Responding to Bob) We're coming right up on it now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: There certainly is a change in the general albedo, particularly in the craters.
The craters are much brighter in their walls than we've seen before.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Although there still is a brown...a light...or a gray dusting over the top of it in here, but it's clearly different - no question about that.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You can't see the contact as you cross it but we know we're coming into something lighter.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We ought to sample the rim of one of these craters when we get our LRV sample, because that's what's distinctly lighter.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: How about a range and bearing, guys?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We're at 3.8 here, and we can sample that rim...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Responding to Bob's request) 083, 4.4, 3.8, and I've been...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: How about right over there, Geno?
Can you get on the rim of that crater?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: No, right to the right there...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: ...and the light material right on the rim?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: But I'm going to have to not give you much of a turn because it's...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Now, if you can swing to the left a little bit and then back...Whoa.
Hope my camera stays on there.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're 083, 4.4, and 3.8; and I've been running about 20 to 25 amps, I think, on both.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We are in the light mantle.
It's not a contrasting light like you might expect, or like we're looking at on the Scarp as the Sun shines on it, but I don't think there's any question.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The craters that penetrate into it are definitely different.
However, the surface texture is unchanged.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We don't want to lose it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (A little annoyed with Bob's request while they are busy) Stand by.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Oops, the bags (the SCB on the accessory staff) don't stay open...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It will after we get a couple of samples in there.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I hope I don't lose my camera.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I can't reach it, or I'd help you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I guess I didn't do what I wanted to do, and that's get that thing (the camera handle) really cinched down.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Boy, Bob, one of the remarkable things is the Sun-angle difference on that light mantle when you're looking at the slopes of the Scarp versus what we're on.
I hate to use a familiar term, but my impression right here is there is more of a raindrop influence than back at the LM, or in the darker mantle.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I think the big thing is, though, that each one of these little craters is much more lightly colored.
But we're still not...There's no crater in view that does...(correcting himself) that has a blocky rim.
There's fragmental rims based on, almost certainly, instant rock, but no blocky rims.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You know, one of the reasons those craters look lighter is because of their Sun angle.
(That is, the Sun striking the) walls of some of these little craters...It's the same material we're driving on, I'll bet.
Yeah, there is instant rock right there, Jack; you're right.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The fragment population is certainly less than 1 percent in here.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: When I say fragments, I'm talking about rocks that are greater than a centimeter in (garbled, probably "average diameter").
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You know, it may be me, Bob; (static) but it also seems to be a little bit more difficult to drive down-Sun in this area.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, I think it is brighter, Geno.
I was thinking that a minute ago, but it's hard to make a (judgment)...I think your normal albedo is greater.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And the little craters still have the central pits.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're losing your comm a little bit, guys.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Static) (Responding to Bob) Well, we're (pointing) right at you.
(Checking the low-gain indicator against the heading indicator) 270.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: They still look like the gabbro, though.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Well, a couple of them looked to me like they had some very light (static) crystals in them.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'm afraid those are zap pits.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I think I've been fooled by that, too, and that's what I estimated the plagioclase (content) by.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Comm clears) I just want to keep you out of those slopes, and I'll tell you, I get you in some...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: ...(by working at) keeping you out of them.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're getting a little more blocks in here.
Of course, we're approaching the dark mantle again.
Now, you can see the difference.
You got to look hard for it!
But, you see those craters out in there are not white anymore.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I got to get around that slope.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You still got Hole-in-the-Wall picked out over there, don't you?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And, I'm trying to keep comm with them as I'm turning here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And, I've been keeping the thing on.
I don't know if they're reading us, but I've been moving it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Read you loud and clear, guys.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Looking up on the South Massif, we've got real good views of the block-strewn fields.
There seems to be two dominant colorations of the rocks.
The light-colored ones (are) very light tan to white, and then there are the blue-gray rocks.

There's one major outcrop of blue-gray about a sixth of the way down the slope, (in) the center of the field-of-view we have; and it looks very much like similar blue-gray rocks right at the crest, the highest point from our vantage point.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, give me a range and bearing, please.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: ...(do) you want another sample of the dark mantle here?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, we want (a range and bearing) as soon as you get into the dark mantle.
We're estimating it's (at a range of) something like 4.3 (km), 4.5, somewhere in that vicinity.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (To Gene) Now, let's...If you can...Okay, right over there, and maybe I can get a rock with it (the soil).
See that batch of rocks there?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And CDR (Commander) is 3.85 (psi suit pressure) and about 70 percent (oxygen remaining) and no (warning) flags.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I got the rock, and there's some dirt in there.
Maybe I'd better get a little bit more dirt.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Pause) You don't have any trouble getting dirt.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And that's primarily a rock fragment.
Jack's getting a soil sample with it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Jack, look at the wrinkles over there on the North Massif.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: There's no question that there are apparent lineations all over these Massifs, in a variety of directions.
Hey, look at how that Scarp goes up the side (of the North Massif) there.
There's a distinct change in texture.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: As a matter of fact, the lineations are not present on the Scarp, that we can see, where it crosses the North Massif.
There is no sign of those lineations on there.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I can see what you're talking about now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It looks like the Scarp overlays the North Massif, doesn't it?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, now I need to get in that bag.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And pray for me, Bob, that I don't lose my camera.
Hanover is quite a ways up the (North Massif) slope.
I don't think we'd have gotten to it, as we planned that time.
But the appearance you have of the Scarp/North Massif contact is one of the Scarp being smoother textured, less cratered, and certainly less lineated.
And I wouldn't be a bit surprised if it's, as Gene says, younger.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: It's not just the slope, it's the materials on the other side of the Scarp, on the west side.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, I'm going to have to really ease up on pictures.
I forgot to give them a frame count.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We didn't get a frame count.
You want to give us a frame count there, Jack?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: The problem is (that) every time I take my hand off, my camera loosens up again.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And our estimate is that if you continue to go between 50 and 100 meters between frames, we'll make it.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Are those Massifs getting to look big now!
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That frame at the LRV sample was about 115.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'll tell you, that Scarp looks nice over there, too, doesn't it?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Pause) Okay, we're back down in our old friend, the dark mantle.
And I think the zero-phase point is not as bright as it was.
(We're) passing a small crater, but the block population is still way down there in about - Whoops, watch that one - (the) one percent (range).
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And, 17, for your benefit, we're showing you with very good net mobility rates here; and things (are) looking quite good.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I'll tell you, it takes all your time to drive, though.
You look around, and you're in a hole.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay here's another small crater (with) instant rock, the same little pit (at the center of the crater) and a spattering of glass holding the pit materials together.
None of the glass linings look very coherent, Bob.
They mainly just seem to be a sprinkling of glass that's coating (pause) the instant rock.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: The craters at about 10 to 15 meters in diameter seem to have somewhat more blocky material in their rims.
But they're not clear-cut blocky rim craters.
And here's one that's probably 50 meters across that has a fair number of blocks in the bottom.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Looks like it (the impact) might have just about gotten down to where the gabbro starts to be abundant again.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay; I copy on that one, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Static; garbled) start heading toward 12 o'clock (west); and I'm going to work my way up to Hole-in-the-Wall and from there on up, right?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It's a very long, very subtle, very gentle slope.
We'll just have to get some more words when we get there.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: How about a range and bearing while you're at it?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I've been making 10 or 12 clicks (kilometers per hour) most of the time.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And about 20 to 22 amps (the electric current to the Rover wheel motors) most of the time.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, we're losing a little bit of low-gain (signal) there, Geno.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I think you need to tilt it up a little.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Well, our pitch angle (the Rover's up/down orientation) changes all the time.
Bob, I have been within 10 to 20 degrees of you the whole time.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, Bob, we're not in light mantle, I don't think.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Gosh, I was going to say the craters are whiter than they have been.
And even the zero-phase point's brighter, too.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Static clears) I think that place where we had those small, blocky craters was within the dark mantle.
They're not evident here in the lighter stuff.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Boy, is that (South Massif) getting big.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That really gives me a strange feeling.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Gives me a strange feeling too.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'm not sure I've got enough guts to make them intentional.
(Pause) Man, everything's getting to look big the closer you get.
(Pause) Hole-in-the-Wall looks more promising, though, Bob.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, I don't think that's going to be any problem.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (At least not) until we get up and look back.
Oh, man, what a trip this is going to be.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That Min cooling is just about right, isn't it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: No, it's just about warm for me.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Bob, is my PLSS cooling working all right?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It looks like it's working to us.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, the rock fragments still look like gabbro.
The craters tend to have white walls and white rims, which they don't have in the dark mantled area.
The block population is way down, (and covers) one percent (of the surface) or less.
However, the bigger craters do have more blocks; but nowhere does that population seem to get above about five percent.
And that's on the walls and the rims of the craters, say bigger than 15 meters.
There's one probably 20 meters in diameter that has some blocks on it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Nemo will be hard to see.
But, yeah, it's probably that one right in there.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Well, it's pretty...Yeah, well...Yeah, that's close to (the) Scarp.
It's probably right off your wing there.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, I'm going straight ahead and then I'm going to make a left turn (into Hole-in-the-Wall).
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Now, Lara...I can see blocks in the northwest rim of Lara.
At least, it's rugged terrain; and it looks like blocky terrain.
It looks like it may be a couple hundred meters in average diameter.
It starts maybe three-quarters of the way up the wall and goes right up to the rim.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Hey, Bob, Hole-in-the-Wall seems to be a...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That central pit goes down about half the depth of the crater, and the crater is a fresh, 3-meter crater.
It almost was a cylindrical pit.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Hey, Bob, Hole-in-the-Wall is just a step, headed down to the south or southeast on the Scarp.
The Scarp is just about what I think we all expected it to be.
It's very rolling and relatively smooth.
I don't really see any outcrops (of rock) exposed anywhere out here to the south.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You see, now there's Station 3 area right up there.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Looks like maybe that set of...See that bigger crater over there to the right of Lara?
That probably is a good place for Station 3.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, we're going to find out something very shortly (about Hole-in-the-Wall).
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It doesn't look very rocky, Gene.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: How about bearing and range, guys?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, I'll give it to you just as soon as I make my turn.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Are...Are you...Are you going to turn over that or go on closer...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: No, I'm going right up straight ahead and then go on to the inside of that place.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Giving a bearing and range to the LM) 081 and 5.6.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: There's...Now the craters are getting very, very light colored in their rims and walls.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You notice when we're in the light mantle looking at the Scarp, at this angle, it loses some of its high albedo (that is, its apparent brightness)?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We've got a long depression to go around.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, Jack, we got to watch it because I got to go around a long depression.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I don't know how I can get over there to...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I may have to go up over there.
I can't go down that hole.
That one's not going to make it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'm going to go over here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: What was your pitch then, Geno?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We'll go up this gentle slope.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (To Houston) We made a turn to the south a little bit at 081 and 5.7.
Are you going to try to drive up there?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I don't think we're going to have any choice.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Looks to me like just to the left of that...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: ...white crater is a...Or even (go) right like you're headed now and then bear up to the right.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Pause) Find out how this (Rover) climbs in a minute.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Oh, I think you're all right.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, Bob, I'm starting up the Scarp at 081, 6.6, and 5.7.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: This is the first tongue of the Scarp.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I don't even think the Rover knows it's going uphill.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, I'll tell you, this Rover doesn't know it's going up a hill.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Looks to me like you may be able to head just like you're going.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Whatever makes up the light mantle is - at least, the instant rock that it forms - is much lighter than anything we see (elsewhere).
Those fragments probably are 30 percent lighter than any fragments we see out on the dark mantle.
And that's around the fresh craters.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, I just wanted to make sure my antenna's working.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're doing a little zig-zag navigation.
I literally came up a slope at about a heading of 240 (WSW).
We couldn't get through the actual turn to the south because there is a big crater right at the foot of it.
So we're just making our way through some relatively local undulating slopes that get pretty steep, but it seems to be no problem.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, I think we're in good shape.
Bob, I can't ...There are not any blocks big enough to really make a statement about what the (underlying) rock is.
But it really doesn't look like gabbro anymore.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And a reminder that eventually you're going to have to turn to the south a little bit to pick up the final thing...at Station 2.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We're not on top of that Scarp, yet.
We're still in the Hole-in-the-Wall rim.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Bob, as far as lineations in the soil or on the surface that are observable at this range (that is, close at hand), I don't see any.
I think there may be a finer raindrop pattern on the light mantle than maybe there was out on the dark.
But that's an awfully hard judgment to make.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Bob, we've slowed down (to) between about 5 to 8 - maybe 5 to 10 - clicks most of the time.
I'm going to head right up there, I think.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Pretty healthy roll we're going to have here.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, I'm going to head more straight up the hill.
Once I get up on top, I'll be all right.
I'm going to head down in this hole and then up that way.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I don't mind pitch, but I sure don't like roll.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Now I'm going to head straight up...I'm going to head straight up that slope right there.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Bob, It looks like maybe the large fragments in here are still crystalline.
They have white zap pits on them.
But they do not yet really resemble the gabbros.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Give us a hack when you get up on top of the Scarp there.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Let me tell you, Bob, I've got to go cross-slope some of the time because the Rover is really working to go uphill now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: As I look up the Scarp to the west, there are some big blocks scattered around on our horizon; but, again, I would guess that we're not dealing with more than 2 or 3 percent total coverage of blocks in here, if that.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Well, I think, for the most part - for the most part - we're on top.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Now, Jack, where was Nansen (Crater) with respect to those (boulder) tracks up there (on South Massif)?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, they never really had any good tracks pinned down, I don't think.
You'll be able to see Nansen, I think, soon as you get over this hill.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Boy, I tell you, when we look back (into the valley toward the LM), that's going to be quite a sight if we can see into that Sun.
Well, I'd say this is the last straw to the top.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'm going to try to get over along (means "to") the base of the massif now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Head towards that track area there, anyway.
(To Bob) There are a lot of boulder tracks coming down from the blue-gray rocks, Bob.
We'll see whether or not we're going to get to those tracks at Nansen, or we might want to move over to the tracks and see if we can find the boulder that made them.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay; if they're in the vicinity, it might be a nice idea.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: But there's no question where those tracks come from.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And we gather you're slowing down to about 5 clicks now, coming up this last rise.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I'm back up to about 7 to 10 now, Bob.
The slowdown is because that's about all it will take!
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Bob, I have the impression that there is a dipping (meaning "not horizontal") zone of blue-gray outcrops or block concentrations up there on the Massif that trends from the high point just beneath the Earth cross-slope; and probably the apparent dip is - oh, I don't know - 10 or 15 degrees (down) to the east.
It looks like those outcrops may match up along that trend.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack, I'm going to head right along this ridge because I think that's the depression we were talking about.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let me get over here, and then I'll head a little bit to the south.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, we're a little more west, I think, than we intended to be.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Bob...(Trying to read the instruments) What is it?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Bob, I've had an impression, and I can't prove it yet, that we're dealing with more heterogeneous rock.
Possibly there are breccias in here.
But it's awfully hard to tell right now.
They're very light-colored rocks, I think even lighter colored than the gabbros.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'm afraid those...I think we can follow those (boulder) tracks.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, I think we can see some of those coming down.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I think the ones from the big outcrop of blue-gray rock, though, are the ones going into Nansen.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Bob, my best guess - let's see, 077, 7.7, 6.6 - is that we're coming up on the northern side of Nansen.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And, let me tell you, this is quite a Rover ride.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: But it's quite a machine, I tell you!
I think it would do a lot more than we'd let it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Pause) I think that big crater up there on the side (of the mountain) is the one that you can see in the photographs, just above Station 2.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I think if I come up here, do a hard left turn; you unbuckle your belt, you'll roll right down into the bottom of Nansen.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And remember we're going to about 068 (bearing) and about 7.4 (range) will be Station 2 (with the actual position being 071/7.6).
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay, there's Nansen over there, huh?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, Bob, I think we're into a breccia population now.
I think the blocks in the light mantle are largely breccias.
The white zaps do not seem to be nearly as apparent.
They tend to be chalky when they get hit.
At least, in the large craters (meaning "large zap pits"), the walls are chalky looking.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Pause) Boy, I tell you, if I hang on to this camera until you stop and can tighten it up, it'll be a miracle.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Bob, how long have we been driving?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We estimate you've got about a kilometer and a half to go - a little over a kilometer, anyway.
Stand by, we'll check on the time.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Man, this has been a trip.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You know, we're really up on top of this thing.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You guys have been driving 64 minutes, and that counts the time to stop and deploy the (explosive) charge (for a seismic experiment) and pick up the Rover samples.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We're very clearly going downhill now, into the trough area that surrounds the Massif, between the mantle and the massif.
But the trough is much greater in extent than just Nansen scale.
I never realized that it was so much of a depression in here.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: How about a range and bearing readout.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I'm not sure we're going to be able to see the LM.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We won't be able to see the LM from down here.
We'll be too low to see it.
Fact is, I don't think I can see that far.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The surface patterns are still the same, Bob.
The main difference being that we're getting probably a gradual increase in block population; and the blocks seem to be of a different character.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And around the crater here that's maybe 75 meters in diameter, there's probably 5 percent blocks - fragments, I should say - greater than a centimeter (in size).
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Boy, look at all the dust without that fender (meaning "with the replacement fender on").
I hate to think of what it would have been like with that (replacement) fender gone.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Pause) There's a good-sized block, sort of blue-gray.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Looking up there, Jack, I ought to get some 500s looking right up that hill, but...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, you may want to do that out a ways (from the Massif).
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Some of that stuff is mantled, or buried, in the massif material.
Some of (the rocks) just seems to be laying on it, of course.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, I think it has to do with how long it's been there.
You'll tend to get the downslope movements forming uphill fillets, and that's what a lot of it looks like.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Most of it is uphill fillets.
Most of it is pretty sharp.
(To Bob) But my guess, from back at the LM, that those blocks on the massif were much more angular, I think, is a good guess because that's what they look like to me here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And looking up into our blue-gray outcrop area, I still have even more the impression that there's a planar orientation that dips off to the southeast.
Maybe just fracturing, but (it's) pretty clear up there, I think.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: The LM (means "the Earth") is now 50 percent of the massif height away from the massif.
I think we will keep it (Earth) on top.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Listen, if the Earth goes behind it, we're changing Station 2.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gonna be nip and tuck, pardon the expression.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: As we get closer, actually, we're out of the block area.
And that blocky region of 5 percent may have been just associated with that crater.
I still see no lineations, although...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Look at these wrinkles (on the Massif), though, Jack...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I was talking about the mantle.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: But you're right about on the Massif.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: The same wrinkled lineations we saw sloping uphill to the west on the eastern half of the Massif are still very evident at this Sun angle (and, therefore, may be real and not an optical illusion).
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, 17; And we're estimating that you should be there within...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: ...about 5 minutes to meet the walkback constraints.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Well, we're...Bob, we're almost ready to park.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Responding to Gene) Well, I wouldn't have gone so far as to say that.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Bob, the boulder tracks are really just chains of small craters, for the most part.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I don't think they can tilt the television camera high enough to see the top of the Massif.
(Pause) Jack, we're on the edge here, but I don't know.
Is that the...Well, let me go up here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: See, there's Nansen off to my right now.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, I just want to make sure that I'm not driving down a hole here...which I am, but...I don't want to drive down(into) Nansen.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The end of Nansen is over there near those blocks.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Unfortunately, the good boulder tracks are over into Nansen.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I think (a) station (pause) just about anywhere near the big blocks...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, that's where I'm going to put it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That's where I'm going to put it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, that's where we're going to make Station 2; right up there.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Boy, you're looking right into Nansen.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're right where we wanted to be for Station 2.
And it looks like a great place.
It looks like quite a bit of variety from here.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, Jack, I'm going to do a 180 (degree turn) and park the Rover at 045.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: There's a blue-gray rock and a lighter-colored tan rock.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: See where they can look in here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Are you going to park it?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Right on the other side of this little crater heading (garbled).
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, Bob you ought to have us again.
(Static fades as Gene re-aligns the antenna) 9.1, 7.6.
Are you reading, by the way?
transcript.speaker: Cernan
